title: Automation Interview Question Bank
subtitle: Intermediate scripting and automation questions focused on practical repeatable workflows.

questions.question: Why is a dry-run mode valuable in automation scripts?
questions.intent: Checks whether you think about safety before real side effects.
options: A. It previews actions without changing real data.
B. It always makes the script faster.
C. It removes the need for logs.
questions.correct_answer: A. It previews actions without changing real data.
questions.explanation: Dry-run mode lets you inspect which files, records, or API calls would be affected before the script writes, deletes, or updates anything.
compare: Dry-run: safer rollout and easier review.
Immediate execution: faster, but riskier for destructive operations.

questions.question: What problem does idempotency solve in scheduled jobs?
questions.intent: Checks whether you can prevent duplicate side effects.
options: A. It makes every job run only once forever.
B. Re-running the same job should not duplicate the final effect.
C. It avoids all network errors.
questions.correct_answer: B. Re-running the same job should not duplicate the final effect.
questions.explanation: Jobs can be retried or triggered twice. Idempotency means the end state remains correct even if the same input is processed more than once.
compare: Idempotent job: safer retries and replays.
Non-idempotent job: duplicate emails, charges, or rows are more likely.

questions.question: When should you retry an operation in automation workflows?
questions.intent: Tests whether you distinguish transient failures from permanent ones.
options: A. Retry temporary failures like timeouts or 503 responses.
B. Retry every error forever.
C. Never retry anything.
questions.correct_answer: A. Retry temporary failures like timeouts or 503 responses.
questions.explanation: Retries are appropriate for transient issues. Permanent failures like invalid credentials or malformed input should usually fail fast and be fixed instead of retried.
compare: Transient error: retry can help.
Permanent error: retry wastes time and can amplify load.

questions.question: Why is `pathlib` usually better than raw path strings in automation code?
questions.intent: Checks practical Python style for file operations.
options: A. It provides clearer cross-platform path operations.
B. It disables all file errors.
C. It only works on Linux.
questions.correct_answer: A. It provides clearer cross-platform path operations.
questions.explanation: pathlib improves readability and reduces manual string concatenation mistakes. It also keeps path logic more portable across operating systems.
questions.code_snippet: from pathlib import Path\nreport = Path('output') / 'summary.json'\nreport.parent.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)
compare: pathlib: explicit path objects and methods.
Raw strings: easy to start, easier to misuse.

questions.question: What should good automation logs include?
questions.intent: Checks whether you know how to debug jobs after they run unattended.
options: A. Context like job id, input source, counts, and error details.
B. Only the word success.
C. Nothing, because logs slow the script.
questions.correct_answer: A. Context like job id, input source, counts, and error details.
questions.explanation: Automation often runs without a human watching. Logs should help answer what ran, what changed, where it failed, and how much work was done.
compare: Structured logs: easier filtering and diagnosis.
Sparse logs: hard to reconstruct incidents.

questions.question: Which approach is usually faster for repeated membership checks on a large collection: `if item in big_list` or `if item in big_set`?
options: A. `big_set`, because average membership lookup is faster.
B. `big_list`, because lists are always optimized better in Python.
C. They are always identical in performance.
questions.correct_answer: A. `big_set`, because average membership lookup is faster.
questions.explanation: Repeated list scans are linear, while set membership is usually near constant time on average. This difference matters heavily in loops.
compare: List membership: simpler but slower at scale for repeated checks.
Set membership: better when uniqueness and fast lookup matter.

questions.question: What is the main optimization problem in opening and closing the same output file inside every loop iteration?
questions.intent: Checks practical file I/O efficiency awareness.
options: A. Repeated open-close operations add unnecessary overhead.
B. Python forbids file writes inside loops.
C. It always corrupts the file immediately.
questions.correct_answer: A. Repeated open-close operations add unnecessary overhead.
questions.explanation: Keeping the file open for the batch or buffering writes is usually more efficient than reopening it for every row.
questions.code_snippet:
with open('out.txt', 'a', encoding='utf-8') as f:
    for row in rows:
        f.write(row + '
')
compare: Open per row: high overhead and noisier code.
Open once: faster and easier to reason about.

questions.question: Why is this risky in automation: `for path in files: path.unlink()` with no preview, backup, or filtering?
questions.intent: Checks whether you think about destructive automation failure modes.
options: A. It can destroy unintended files if the input set is wrong.
B. It is safe because Python validates business intent automatically.
C. It only fails when running on Windows.
questions.correct_answer: A. It can destroy unintended files if the input set is wrong.
questions.explanation: Delete operations need guardrails like dry-run, folder scoping, confirmation, or output review because mistakes are hard to reverse.
questions.code_snippet: for path in candidate_paths:\n    print(path)  # preview first\n# delete only after verification
compare: Blind delete: fast but dangerous.
Previewed delete: slower upfront but operationally safer.

questions.question: What is the trap in retrying a non-idempotent action like sending an email or charging a card without deduplication?
options: A. The action may happen multiple times and create duplicate side effects.
B. Retries automatically become idempotent after the second attempt.
C. Python's exception handling prevents duplicates.
questions.correct_answer: A. The action may happen multiple times and create duplicate side effects.
questions.explanation: Retries are safe only when the final effect tolerates repeats or you add idempotency keys, deduplication, or state checks.
compare: Blind retry: can duplicate external effects.
Idempotent retry: safer under timeouts and transient failure.

questions.question: Why can a cron job that takes longer than its schedule interval become dangerous?
options: A. Overlapping runs can race, duplicate work, or overload dependencies.
B. Cron automatically serializes every job perfectly.
C. It only affects log formatting.
questions.correct_answer: A. Overlapping runs can race, duplicate work, or overload dependencies.
questions.explanation: Long jobs may still be running when the next schedule starts. Locks, lease files, or queue-based orchestration can prevent harmful overlap.
compare: No overlap control: simple but fragile.
Locking or orchestration: safer for recurring workloads.

questions.question: Which is usually more memory efficient for very large files: `rows = f.readlines()` or iterating `for line in f`?
options: A. `for line in f`, because it streams incrementally.
B. `readlines()`, because it compresses lines automatically.
C. They use the same memory for any file size.
questions.correct_answer: A. `for line in f`, because it streams incrementally.
questions.explanation: Streaming avoids loading the entire file into memory at once, which is critical for large logs and exports.
questions.code_snippet: with open('big.log', encoding='utf-8') as f:\n    for line in f:\n        process(line)
compare: readlines(): easy for small files, memory-heavy for large ones.
Streaming iteration: better for large-file automation.

questions.question: Why should automation scripts often validate input schema before processing thousands of rows?
questions.intent: Checks whether you front-load failure detection.
options: A. So the script fails early instead of producing partial bad output.
B. Because validation makes loops disappear.
C. Only because tests require it.
questions.correct_answer: A. So the script fails early instead of producing partial bad output.
questions.explanation: Schema checks catch missing columns, invalid types, or broken sources before deep side effects happen.
compare: Early validation: safer and clearer failures.
Late discovery: partial work and harder cleanup.

questions.question: What is the likely bottleneck in calling an API once per row when many rows share the same lookup key?
options: A. Repeated network calls for identical information.
B. Python cannot loop over repeated values.
C. The garbage collector always blocks requests.
questions.correct_answer: A. Repeated network calls for identical information.
questions.explanation: Memoization or pre-grouping repeated keys often reduces latency and API cost dramatically.
compare: Naive repeated fetch: simple but expensive.
Cached lookup: better for repeated keys.

questions.question: Why is using local machine time without timezone awareness a trap in scheduled automations?
options: A. Timezone and daylight changes can shift execution assumptions.
B. Python stores all dates in UTC automatically.
C. Cron ignores timezone differences entirely.
questions.correct_answer: A. Timezone and daylight changes can shift execution assumptions.
questions.explanation: Scheduling logic becomes fragile when business time and server time are mixed carelessly.
questions.code_snippet: from datetime import datetime, timezone\nnow = datetime.now(timezone.utc)
compare: Naive local time: easier to start, riskier in real operations.
Explicit timezone handling: safer and more portable.

questions.question: Which is usually better for joining many output lines: repeated `text += line` inside a loop or collecting into a list and using `'\n'.join(...)`?
options: A. Collecting and joining is usually better for many pieces.
B. Repeated concatenation is always faster in Python.
C. They are identical for large batches.
questions.correct_answer: A. Collecting and joining is usually better for many pieces.
questions.explanation: Repeated string concatenation can be inefficient as the text grows, while join is designed for assembling many fragments.
compare: Repeated `+=`: okay for tiny cases, worse at scale.
Join: better for larger assembled output.
